content:
Welcome back to ICT Mentorship Core Content
- Month 10: Commodity Seasonal Tendencies
- My
Personal Favorites.
This is a commodity market discussion. It's important to understand that everything discussed here is
specifically for paper trading only. I am not a CTA or a commodity trade advisor.

Panaceas and 'be all, end alls' do not exist in trading, and seasonal tendencies are certainly not one. While
there's validity behind them, due to human error and the seasonal tendency not always taking effect, you
can lose money if you place all your faith on them.
My personal interpretation is that seasonal tendencies act like a roadmap or a treasure map, giving a
normal expectation of what should be seen in price action at that time of year. However, anything can
happen; for agriculturals, there could be a drought (short supply, high demand, prices rise) or a bumper
crop (high supply, low demand).
They are not fantasies, but good roadmaps for what to expect for each individual commodity.

Each slide will present the seasonal tendency on the left and a weekly chart over the last five years on the
right, allowing you to form your own opinion on their validity. Through study of old data, you will likely
conclude there's something going on that provides validity.
Seasonal tendencies offer insight. If the market is not trading in line with the seasonal tendency, it provides
valuable information. For instance, if the soybean market should be bullish from February to June but is
trading lower, it means seasonal influences have no impact, and there's likely an underlying large supply of
soybeans, preventing higher prices. Conversely, if bullishness is expected and occurs, supported by a
primary bull market, the seasonal tendency can support a sustained move.

The soybean market generally experiences a bullish period from the first of February going into the June
time period. After this, around June-July, a seasonal high is typically created, followed by a trade down into
the September-October time period, which usually forms another seasonal low.

section_title: COMMODITY FUTURES CONTRACTS AND CHART LINES
content:
At the top of every seasonal tendency chart, a yellow box indicates the delivery contracts, moving from left
to right (e.g., Mar, May, July, Aug, Sep, Nov, Jan for soybeans). When one contract expires, you trade the
next month's contract.
Always try to trade the first or nearby month, as this is where most open interest and volume reside. For
instance, some commodities like gold might skip a month in the latter part of the year. To easily determine
the active contract, check barchart.com for open interest and volume; the one with the highest values is
usually the contract to focus on.
On the seasonal tendency charts, there are two lines:
- A solid black line delineates 40 years of price data, showing the long-term seasonal pattern.
- A dashed red line plots a 15-year seasonal tendency pattern.
The purpose of comparing these two timeframes is that a strong seasonal tendency should be evident in
both the shorter-term 15-year data and the longer-term 40-year data. When both lines move in lockstep to
the same direction, agreeing to create a high or low and sustained moves, it signifies a strong, reliable
seasonal tendency.

For soybeans, we observe both the 40-year (solid black) and 15-year (dashed red) lines agreeing on the
following tendencies:
- A rally from the first of February into the mid-portion of the year (June or July).
- A high created in June-July, followed by a trade down into September-October, forming another seasonal
low.
On the right-hand side of the slides, no markups are provided to avoid implying that these seasonal
tendencies are absolute or to 'over-prove' them. The suggestion is to examine the data yourself and
determine its validity. It's recommended to look beyond just the last five years of data shown and go back
as far as possible to see how consistently these patterns materialize.
Observing the weekly chart for soybeans:
- In January 2013, a low was formed, and price rallied into July, consistent with the seasonal tendency.
From July, it traded down into the fall (September-October) creating a seasonal low in October.
- In January 2014, another rally occurred, extending into June, forming a high, then trading down into the
September-October low.
- In 2016, the seasonal influence for price to rally took effect, making a high in June, then trading lower to
an October low.

For a developing commodity trader, the strong seasonal tendencies for soybeans suggest three general
approaches:
- Look for long positions from February going into June-July.
- Look for short positions from June-July down into September-October.
- Look for long positions from September-October going into the following summer months.
This longer bullish phase from fall into summer may break up into two stages or 'legs' in price action. This
is how I interpret and utilize seasonal tendencies, and it has served me well for over two decades.

section_title: CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE WHEAT SEASONAL TENDENCIES
content:
The Chicago Board of Trade wheat market has a seasonal tendency that can be 'funky' but is solid when
present. It typically shows a decline in the first half of the year, making a low in the second half of June and
into August, providing a four-to-six-week window for the low to form.
Looking at the weekly chart for July, a rally is generally observed. For example, in 2012, wheat rallied.
However, in 2013 and 2014, the seasonal influence for bullishness was not present, and prices declined.
Similarly, 2016 did not show bullishness, and in 2017 (at the time of recording), the market was in a strong
bearish trend.
Conversely, examining influences for sell-offs from January down into June:
- January 2013 saw a high form around $8 a bushel, and price traded lower into July.
- January 2014 only declined for about a month before rallying.
- January 2015 saw a decline into May, where a small low formed before a rally began.
- 2016 experienced a decline, with a retracement above $5 before trading down to a low in the July-August
period.
- January 2017 did not see a decline, but rather a rally, indicating times when the seasonal tendency might
not be significant.

Even when seasonal tendencies lack significance, they provide insight. If the wheat market enters a bull
market, the best times to be a buyer, based on historical price action, are:
- Looking for a low in the July-August time period.
- Looking for a low in November.
These are the 'best times' to buy wheat. Conversely, if wheat is in a bearish market, as seen in recent
examples, the best times to look for short entries are at the beginning of the year, extending into the
summer months.

section_title: CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE CORN SEASONAL TENDENCIES
content:
For Chicago Board of Trade corn, the best time to look for short opportunities, as indicated by the seasonal
tendency chart, is in May and June. Historically, over the last 40 years, May and June create seasonal
tendencies for corn to drop, usually declining into the September-October period where it makes a
seasonal low.
To verify this, we look at the April timeframe on the weekly chart and observe if it declines into September
- October:
- In 2013, after a slight retracement, corn fell significantly from July into the October timeframe.
- In April 2014, it created the very high of that year and traded down into the September-October low.

In 2016, we observed a low forming in the September period, consistent with expectations. Corn has since
been moving sideways to slightly higher. In 2017, there was no notable decline in April-May; corn actually
went higher.
This behavior suggests corn might be a 'sleeper market,' meaning it's not abiding by its seasonal tendency
for a spring decline. When a market acts contrary to its seasonal tendency, especially by showing strength
during an expected weak period, it indicates underlying relative strength.

Feeder cattle, the source of hamburgers, typically makes a seasonal low in the April-May time period. This
low can sometimes occur as early as February, as shown by the dashed red line (15-year data). However,
the strongest moves higher, particularly observed in the dashed line, begin at the end of April, extending
into May. A seasonal high is generally formed around August and September, followed by a period of
consolidation.
This information suggests that it's an ideal time to be a buyer in the spring and sell positions as the market
moves into the fall.
Examining the weekly chart:
- In 2013, from the April delineation, the market rallied all the way up into the October high, consistent with
the seasonal tendency, then consolidated.
- In 2014, a rally occurred, extending into October, forming the very high of the feeder cattle market for that
year.

section_title: FEEDER CATTLE EXAMPLES AND MARKET DYNAMICS
content:
- In April 2015, price rallied again but fell prematurely in August, although the impulse for higher prices after
a long-term market structure breakdown was still observed.
- In 2016, the seasonal tendency had no impact due to the overall bearishness in feeder cattle.
- In 2017, April created a significant short-term low at the $1.30 mark and subsequently rallied up to $1.60,
a phenomenal price move. Each 100 basis points (one full point or handle) is worth $500 per contract for
feeder cattle. The market might still go higher, but the rally from $1.30 to $1.60 has been substantial.

Live cattle, the living animals before processing, typically show a seasonal high forming in the February
- March time period, and a seasonal low forming in June. The stronger impulse is often seen with the June
low.
This usually leads to a markup period in the middle of the year, extending into the end of the year. To utilize
this, look for a July low on the weekly chart and anticipate a rally into the January time period.
Observing the weekly chart:
- In July 2012, a rally did occur, extending into January 2013.
- In June 2013, a rally also developed, continuing into the end of 2013 and early 2014.
- In June 2014, the market created the low of the year and rallied significantly, creating the high of the year
in what appears to be November.

- In 2015, the market was in a bear market, and seasonal influences had no impact during the June-July
period. However, the sell-offs in February-March of 2015, 2016, and 2017 aligned with the market's
bearishness, creating nice short opportunities and market tops.
- A significant low formed in October 2016, which doesn't seasonally fit, but the market began to trade
higher.
- In 2017, there was a decline in February-March, extending into April. But then, April, May, and June saw
the effects of the seasonal tendency with higher prices in cattle.
This is a strong seasonal tendency, especially when combined with technical analysis, proving to be very
powerful.

Lean Hogs (formerly pork bellies) exhibits one of the closest things to perfection in seasonal tendencies
that I have found and studied over historical data.
The pattern is:
- A buying opportunity in March-April.
- A high forming in May-June.
- A seasonal low occurring in September-October.
Primarily, if you're looking to buy Lean Hogs, target the February-March time period. On the weekly chart,
find April and look one vertical line to its left (March) for a seasonal low.

section_title: LEAN HOGS EXAMPLES AND MARKET DYNAMICS
content:
Examining the weekly chart:
- In March 2013, the low was clearly hit, leading to a rally into July, creating the seasonal high, and then a
fall.
- In February-March 2014, a phenomenal opportunity emerged as the market rallied vertically and insanely
high a 'Millionaire Maker' or 'mega trade.' It precisely hit the seasonal tendency high in July.
- In March 2015, the seasonal tendency again led to a rally into June-July, forming the high, followed by a
fall.
- The following year (2016), the March-April period again created a seasonal low, rallying into June-July to
form the high, selling off into September-October.
- In 2017, the March-April seasonal tendency for Lean Hogs to rally has occurred in stunning fashion.
This is one of the 'best kept secrets' for professional commodity traders, particularly those in agriculturals,
who trade this pattern every year.

Cocoa, used in hot chocolate and candies, has a seasonal tendency that, while sometimes 'muddy,' shows
consistent patterns:
- A low forms in May-June.
- A rally occurs into the September-October time period.
Specifically, a seasonal high is expected to form during the last week of August into September, and a
seasonal low during the last week of May into the first week of June. To apply this, find July on your chart,
then go one vertical line to the left (June) to look for the seasonal low, anticipating a trade up into the
October timeframe.

Observing the weekly chart:
- In June 2012, a rally occurred that traded up into the September time period.
- This pattern recurred in 2013, 2014, and 2015.
- The rally in 2015 was the subject of a case study during a live YouTube session, focusing on a buy that
traded up to the $3200-$3250 level. Although it didn't trade higher long-term, it reached the target, resulting
in options doubling and tripling in value on a paper trade.
- In 2017, the same influence was observed, with Cocoa rallying from the $1800 level up to almost the
$2100 level, consistent with the seasonal tendency.

Orange juice exhibits a strong seasonal tendency for a September-October low to form, followed by a rally.
The easiest way to apply this is to find October on the weekly chart, look for a low, and see if it rallies into
December or roughly one vertical line to the left of the January mark.
Observing the weekly chart:
- This rally occurred in 2012.
- It occurred in 2013.
- In 2014, it occurred one month late.
- It was 'nailed' in 2015 and 2016. We will need to wait and see if it does so in 2017.

Coffee, a market I enjoyed trading when more active in commodities, can move significantly when it moves.
The best time to buy coffee is typically in June-July. Over the last 15 years, there's been a seasonal
tendency to create a low in mid-June, while historically (40-year data), it's usually the month of July.
Observing the weekly chart:
- A low was created in July 2012.
- It beautifully nailed a low in July 2014.
- It did a wonderful job in 2016. It did not create as much of a low in 2013 or 2015. We anticipate seeing
one occur in 2017, so we will be watching the coffee market in the coming month.

section_title: COFFEE BEARISH SEASONAL TENDENCIES AND COTTON INTRODUCTION
content:
For bearishness in coffee, the ideal scenario is to look for an April-May high. During bearish market
conditions:
- April-May 2013 saw a nice sell-off.
- A high was created in April-May 2014, leading to a rather nice sell-off.
- April-May 2015, while bearish, created a nice sell-off that took time but moved lower.
- 2016 had a small retracement lower, nothing significant.
- In 2017, the April-May period has created bearishness for the coffee market, which is reflected in price
action.
This concludes the discussion for the agricultural commodities. The next seasonal tendency is for cotton.

Cotton shows a very strong tendency to sell off in the May time period. To identify this, look at the April
portion of the weekly chart for any sell-offs.
Examining the weekly chart:
- In 2013, there was a really nice sell-off for cotton, dropping from 94 cents a pound down to 80 cents a
14-cent move. Each full handle (cent) is $500 for this commodity.

- In 2014, cotton created the high of the year in April and sold off rather precipitously, many, many handles
lower.
- In 2015, after such a large move in 2014, there was some consolidation, as expected. However, it still had
a sell-off that moved approximately 400 basis points, or $2000.
- In 2016, we did not see a significant high or sell-off in April. Instead, a low formed, and the market began
to rally. This indicates an opposing force, showing strong underlying strength in the commodity, contrary to
the expected bearishness in the spring seasonal tendency. The market has been moving straight up since
then.
- In 2017, we again did not see a significant sell-off of any importance in April; only about 300 basis points
from 78 to 74 cents, followed by a precipitous rally towards 87 cents a pound.

For financials, crude oil exhibits a strong tendency at the beginning of the year to rally, creating a seasonal
high in the September-October time period.
Observing the weekly chart:
- In January 2013, it initially sold off a little, not quite following the seasonal tendency, but a decline was
seen in the September-October period, creating the high.
- In January 2014, the crude oil market did rally, trading up into June of that year, creating the high, and
then selling off rather aggressively.
- In 2015, a seasonal tendency for a rally was observed, doing so for about 1500 basis points. Later in
2015, the September-October seasonal tendency created a high around the $50 a barrel mark, trading
down into the seasonal low expected in January 2016. Price then rallied all the way up into the seasonally
outlined pattern to the left, delivered perfectly.

section_title: CRUDE OIL INTERPRETATION AND HIGH-GRADE COPPER INTRODUCTION
content:
In 2017, price consolidated at the beginning of the year and then slowly dropped lower. There has been no
seasonal tendency support for bullishness in the first half of this year; instead, price has been going lower.
This indicates a lot of supply in the oil market, suggesting oil will likely trade lower due to the absence of
expected upside momentum.
The next commodity is high-grade copper.

High-grade copper, like wheat, can be 'iffy.' The best times to be buying copper are the months of June and
November, while the best times for selling are September and April.
Examining the weekly chart for selling (April high):
- April 2013 saw a sell-off.
- April 2015 saw a sell-off.
- April 2016 saw a sell-off.
- April 2017 saw a small sell-off.
Examining the weekly chart for buying (June low):
- In 2012, June (to the left of July) created a low, leading to a seasonal tendency long.
- In 2013, a seasonal tendency long in June came to fruition.
- In 2014, there was also a small long in copper.
- In 2015, the seasonal low formation or buy expected in June-July did not occur, but the seasonal
tendency for bearishness in September did come into effect with a sell-off.

- In April 2016, there was a small sell-off, but copper consolidated and remained in a range for most of
2016. It finally rallied in the October-November time period of 2016.
- In April 2017, the seasonal tendency for a decline was again observed.
When copper enters a premium (which will be discussed in a later lesson) and is bullish, coupled with
seasonal tendencies indicating June and November as good buying times, this market can perform
extremely well and move significantly.

Gold is a very simple market, primarily a buyer's market. We look for the July-August low to form, and that's
generally it. While you could look for a sell-off in January-February if you're strongly inclined for a short, the
ideal scenario is to look for long positions.
The reason for this July-August low is tied to the annual jewelry market cycle:
- Gold needs to be mined, stored, and processed.
- It's then turned into jewelry.
- It's brought to market early enough for holiday sales (Christmas, New Year).
This demand cycle dictates the seasonal tendency for gold.

To test this, we look for July to create the lows on the weekly chart:
- In July 2012, there was a rally in gold.
- In July 2013, we saw a rally.
- In 2014, a high formed.
- In July 2015, a rally occurred.
- In June-July 2016, a rally occurred, eventually making a high.
July 2017 is approaching, so we will observe if a rally occurs in gold then.

The silver market, much like gold, typically sees a July-August time period for a low to form. While silver
can also present a selling point in February, our primary focus is on being a buyer.
This is particularly relevant because silver tends to enter parabolic bull markets every 10 to 12 years.
Based on long-term observations, silver appears poised to make a significant run, potentially reaching
$27-28 in the next 18 months or so. My bullish stance during this mentorship stems from this potential,
though it could certainly go higher than $28 if it gets on a strong rip.

If we anticipate the seasonal tendency for silver to occur in the July-August time period, we look for a July
low to form. Examining the weekly chart for all Julys:
- July 2012 saw a low.
- July 2013 saw a low.
- In 2014, a high formed.
- In 2015, there was a smaller consolidation.
- In 2016, price rallied up and then created a high in July.
July 2017 is imminent, so we will observe if that bullish impulse sends silver higher.

This concludes the discussion on seasonal tendencies for these specific commodities. Other commodities,
such as currencies and their relation to Forex, and bond/debt instruments, were covered earlier in this
mentorship.
These are my personal favorite markets, the ones I've historically kept in my basket to observe. There are
many other commodities available to trade, including canola, rice, butter, milk futures, palladium, platinum,
natural gas, and various indices (which will be discussed in next week's teaching for June 2017 content).
In the early 90s, I used to employ quirky techniques with thinner markets like canola, rice, and oats. I would
look for open interest declines in these markets and erroneously attribute them as supporting evidence for
bullishness or bearishness in the broader grain complex (wheat, soybeans, corn). Over the years, I've
learned that this wasn't true; it simply coincided with a general drought in the 90s that caused all grains to
rise. Open interest is directly related only to that particular market, not an idea to be shared across
commodities.

section_title: FINAL ADVICE ON USING SEASONAL TENDENCIES (INTEGRATION)
content:
These seasonal tendencies, in my opinion, have held up very well over the last 20-plus years. I encourage
you to come to your own conclusion regarding their validity. The best way to use them is to integrate them
with all the other concepts taught in this mentorship.
Every year, program reminders into your calendar or smartphone for the beginning of each month. List the
seasonal tendency influences you expect for that particular month. When you review your charts, you'll
know, 'Seasonally, this is a good time for this market to go higher or lower.' Then, delve into your analysis;
don't force it, but seek reasons to justify that seasonal tendency.

Here's a wonderful takeaway: if a market is not following its seasonal tendency, it's telling you something
significant. It could be entering a long-term consolidation phase, or if it's moving in the opposite direction, it
indicates very significant underlying strength or demand for that commodity. For instance, if lower prices
are seasonally expected, but the market is rising, it shows strong demand.
In such cases, check for net long positions by commercials, as discussed earlier in this month's content on
hedging programs. Wait for them to enter a hedging buy program, then test your discount arrays to see if
you can capitalize on any movement. Hopefully, you've learned something from this teaching. These graphs
have profoundly blessed my understanding of the marketplace over two decades, and I'm confident they
will serve you equally well, if not better, with diligent study. Until the next lesson, I wish you good luck and
good trading.
